If we are just defining the protocol and not the object that implements it, we could just not expect the callback called by the implementation to be called synchronously, right? Similarly to how for thenables we assume that the callback could be called asynchronously, but custom thenables can still call it synchronously
bakkot: lol maybe "task cancellation"?
In the design I was envisioning, we'd be specifying it fully, but in principle we could leave it up to implementations I guess
but, given that all existing implementations do call it synchronously, I think that would end up being a de-facto requirement anyway, so it seems silly not to write it down
One reason is that promises have two possible "done" states while abort signals only have one
So the only two that could even make sense are .any and .all, and not the rest

I could imagine .all being useful in some scenarios (e.g. a multiplayer system, where you want to cancel a request only when every requester cancels) but I also have never encountered one in real life
abort signals have two states... possibly three: Pending and Aborted/Triggered are the two definite states... Never is potentially a third.
Never can, in some cases, be considered a second "done" state... only reason I mention
Is there an API exposed that makes it differentiable from pending?
https://github.com/tc39/proposal-error-stack-accessor/issues/9

This sounds more like a `CountdownEvent` (C#)/`CountDownLatch` (Java)/`countdown_latch` (Rust)/etc. and is maybe a bit outside of the purview of cancellation and more aligned with general async and/or thread coordination primitives.
content.formatted_body: This sounds more like a CountdownEvent (C#)/CountDownLatch (Java)/countdown_latch (Rust)/etc. and is maybe a bit outside of the purview of cancellation and more aligned with general async and/or thread coordination primitives.

content.body: certainly you could build it on top of a semaphore, yes
Semaphore is a slightly different concept. Semaphore lets up to _n_ actors in until all spots are filled. Countdown keeps up to _n_ actors out until all spots are filled.
content.formatted_body: Semaphore is a slightly different concept. Semaphore lets up to n actors in until all spots are filled. Countdown keeps up to n actors out until all spots are filled.

Converting 1.50miles to cm is 241401.6 cm (if infinite precision). But really, it's 241000cm when keeping sig figs. Certainly not 241000.00 and keeping the number of fractional digits.
* Converting 1.50miles to cm is 241401.6 cm (if infinite precision). But really, it's 241000cm when keeping sig figs. Certainly not 241000.00 or 241401.60 and keeping the number of fractional digits.

m.new_content.body: Converting 1.50miles to cm is 241401.6 cm (if infinite precision). But really, it's 241000cm when keeping sig figs. Certainly not 241000.00 or 241401.60 and keeping the number of fractional digits.

content.body: I think regardless how you define what the supplied precision is and how it is transformed across conversions and roundings, it will always be confusing in some of the usecases.
Personally I think less is more here, since the interpretation will be super domain specific.
How about the other example of converting 1 1/16 inches (1.0625 in) to mm?
How precise is 1/16th?
That's the question. The point I'm making is that the answer to that question is not apparent from the numerical values themselves.
But the precision is specified when creating the amount? I agree that it should be specified when constructing the amount directly, because our fp numbers don't carry real precision.
I would say 2.41e5 cm? I always learned trailing zeroes are sigfigs
This would be ok with me
I'd rather see 0s than scientific, but they interpretation is the same to me.
